title: severe_winter_storm_response
description: A severe blizzard with whiteout conditions and sub-zero temperatures overwhelms emergency services. Responders must manage multiple cold injury casualties, a multi-vehicle pileup, and a vulnerable population shelter operation simultaneously.

timeline.title: Blizzard Conditions — State of Emergency Declared
timeline.description: Whiteout conditions, −20°C wind chill. State of emergency declared. All non-emergency travel banned.

timeline.title: Multi-Vehicle Pileup Reported
timeline.description: 40-vehicle pileup on the interstate. Multiple injuries. Emergency vehicles responding in difficult conditions.

timeline.title: Power Outage — Vulnerable Population
timeline.description: Ice accumulation causes widespread power outage. 2,000 residents without heat, including nursing home and dialysis centre.

timeline.title: Hypothermia Cases Mounting
timeline.description: EMS reports rising hypothermia presentations at warming centres. Carbon monoxide incidents from improper heating reported.

timeline.title: Shelter Capacity Reached
timeline.description: Emergency warming shelters at 120% capacity. Additional site being activated with Red Cross support.

timeline.title: Dialysis Centre Generator Fails
timeline.description: Dialysis centre backup generator has failed. Patients requiring treatment within 4 hours must be transported.

node-1.title: State of Emergency — Initial Actions
node-1.situation: A severe blizzard with wind chills of −20°C has caused whiteout conditions. A state of emergency has been declared. A 40-vehicle pileup is reported on the interstate with unknown casualties. A power outage has left 2,000 residents without heat, including a nursing home with 80 residents. Emergency warming shelters are being opened. You are the Emergency Management Coordinator.
node-1.question: How do you prioritise your initial response?

options.label: A
options.text: Activate the EOC; dispatch EMS and fire to the pileup with safety officers for responder protection in extreme cold; coordinate immediate evacuation of the nursing home to a heated shelter; activate all warming centres and notify the public via emergency alert.

options.label: B
options.text: Focus all resources on the pileup — it is the most immediate mass casualty event.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait for conditions to improve before deploying EMS — responder safety is paramount.

node-2.title: Multi-Vehicle Pileup — MCI in Extreme Cold
node-2.situation: EMS and fire arrive at the 40-vehicle pileup. Whiteout conditions make scene assessment difficult. Casualties include: 4 patients in cardiac arrest (hypothermic), 12 with moderate-to-severe hypothermia, 9 with trauma injuries, 18 with minor injuries. Ambient temperature is −15°C with strong wind. Responders are at risk of cold injury if operations extend beyond 20 minutes without warming.
node-2.question: How do you manage the pileup MCI in extreme cold?

options.label: A
options.text: Establish a heated treatment area inside a large transport vehicle or bus; apply active rewarming to hypothermic patients; rotate responders through warming cycles every 20 minutes; prioritise cardiac arrest patients for ALS with aggressive rewarming — in hypothermia, patients are not dead until warm and dead.

options.label: B
options.text: Declare hypothermic cardiac arrest patients deceased at the scene — standard resuscitation criteria apply.

options.label: C
options.text: Treat and transport patients outdoors to avoid the time needed to establish a heated area.

node-3.title: Nursing Home Evacuation
node-3.situation: The nursing home with 80 residents has been without heat for 2 hours. Interior temperature is dropping by 2°C per hour and is now at 14°C. Many residents are elderly and frail; several are bedbound or wheelchair-dependent. You have two accessible transport buses with heating and a school bus without heating. The nearest shelter is 8 km away.
node-3.question: How do you conduct the evacuation?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the heated accessible buses for the bedbound and wheelchair-dependent residents first; use the school bus only for ambulatory residents with blankets and maximised vehicle heating; notify the shelter to pre-warm receiving areas and have medical staff ready.

options.label: B
options.text: Evacuate the ambulatory residents first since they can board quickly, then return for the bedbound.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep residents in place — the building retains some heat and evacuation in blizzard conditions is dangerous.

node-4.title: Carbon Monoxide Incidents
node-4.situation: Paramedics report two separate households using gas ovens and a charcoal grill indoors for heating. At the first household, two adults are found unconscious with cherry-red skin; CO levels measure 800 ppm inside. At the second household, three ambulatory patients complain of headache and confusion. Both households are 1.5 km from the warming shelter.
node-4.question: How do you manage these CO incidents?

options.label: A
options.text: Remove all patients immediately to fresh air; apply 100% high-flow oxygen to all patients; transport the unconscious patients urgently to a hospital with hyperbaric oxygen capability; transport the symptomatic ambulatory patients to EMS for assessment and continued oxygen therapy; issue a public advisory about indoor heating dangers.

options.label: B
options.text: Transport all patients to the warming shelter — warmth and fresh air there will resolve their symptoms.

options.label: C
options.text: Treat the unconscious patients only — the ambulatory patients with mild symptoms will recover with fresh air.

node-5.title: Dialysis Centre Generator Failure
node-5.situation: The dialysis centre's backup generator has failed. Eight patients are mid-treatment and 14 more are due within the next 4 hours. Without dialysis, patients in end-stage renal failure can experience life-threatening complications within hours. Roads are partially passable but still hazardous. The nearest operational dialysis centre is 22 km away.
node-5.question: What is the correct emergency response for the dialysis centre?

options.label: A
options.text: Contact the hospital to accept the dialysis patients for emergency treatment; transport mid-treatment patients first with ALS monitoring; coordinate a second generator from emergency management; notify all 14 upcoming patients to proceed to the hospital dialysis unit.

options.label: B
options.text: Wait for the generator to be repaired before transporting — road conditions are too dangerous.

options.label: C
options.text: Transport only the patients already mid-treatment — the others have time and roads may improve.

node-6.title: Scenario Complete
node-6.situation: All four hypothermic cardiac arrest patients were transported with aggressive rewarming — two survived with full neurological recovery. The nursing home was successfully evacuated with no resident fatalities. The two CO-poisoned unconscious patients were treated with hyperbaric oxygen and survived. All eight mid-treatment dialysis patients were safely transported and continued care at the hospital. A second generator arrived at the dialysis centre within 3 hours. The blizzard response is transitioning to recovery operations.
